title: Density — why a kilogram of feathers takes up so much more space
examTip: Always check your units — examiners may give mass in grams and volume in cm³, expecting an answer in kg/m³. Know how to convert: 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³.
introTips: A kilogram of feathers and a kilogram of iron have the same mass — but the feathers fill a room and the iron fits in your hand.
Density is why ships made of steel can float — it's the shape and what's inside that matters, not just the material.
This topic links directly to the required practical — you'll measure density in the lab.
intro: Why does a piece of wood float while a nail sinks, even if the nail is lighter? Why is a bag of flour so much heavier than a bag of popcorn the same size? The answer is density — a measure of how much mass is packed into a given volume. Density is one of the most useful physical quantities in science: it tells you what things are made of, predicts whether they'll float, and explains why the atmosphere gets thinner the higher you go. The particle model gives us a perfect explanation of why different substances have different densities.

keyPoints.heading: What density is and why it matters
keyPoints.content: Density measures how much mass is packed into a given volume. The equation is: ρ = m / V Where: ρ = density (kg/m³ or g/cm³), m = mass (kg or g), V = volume (m³ or cm³) A substance with high density has particles that are either heavier, more closely packed, or both. Some key densities: - Air: ~1.2 kg/m³ - Water: 1000 kg/m³ (1 g/cm³) - Ice: 917 kg/m³ (less than water — so ice floats) - Aluminium: 2700 kg/m³ - Iron: 7900 kg/m³ - Gold: 19,300 kg/m³ - Osmium (densest natural element): 22,590 kg/m³ Floating and sinking: An object will float if its density is less than the density of the fluid it is placed in. An object will sink if it is denser than the fluid. Ice (917 kg/m³) floats on water (1000 kg/m³) — this is why icebergs float and why lakes freeze from the top down, preserving aquatic life beneath.
writeBullets: Density (ρ) = mass (m) ÷ volume (V), unit: kg/m³ or g/cm³
High density — particles are heavier, more closely packed, or both
Water: 1000 kg/m³; Air: ~1.2 kg/m³; Iron: 7900 kg/m³; Gold: 19,300 kg/m³
Object floats if density < fluid density; sinks if density > fluid density
Ice (917 kg/m³) is less dense than water (1000 kg/m³) — so ice floats

keyTerms.def: Mass per unit volume — how much mass is packed into a given space. Symbol: ρ (rho). Unit: kg/m³
The density equation: density equals mass divided by volume

keyPoints.memTip: ρ = m/V — think 'rho equals mass over volume'. Rearrange using the triangle: cover what you want, the rest is the calculation.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often mix up mass and density. Reinforce: a large object can have low density (balloon) and a small object can have high density (lead weight).

keyPoints.heading: Calculating density — worked examples
keyPoints.content: The density equation and its rearrangements: ρ = m/V   m = ρV   V = m/ρ Worked example 1 — will it float? A block of pine wood has mass 600 g and volume 1000 cm³. ρ = m/V = 600/1000 = 0.6 g/cm³ = 600 kg/m³ Since 600 kg/m³ < 1000 kg/m³ (water), the wood floats. ✓ Worked example 2 — finding mass from density and volume A gold ring has volume 0.8 cm³. Density of gold = 19.3 g/cm³. m = ρV = 19.3 × 0.8 = 15.4 g Worked example 3 — unit conversion A substance has density 2.7 g/cm³. Convert to kg/m³. 2.7 g/cm³ × 1000 = 2700 kg/m³ (aluminium) Unit conversion rule: 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³ To convert g/cm³ → kg/m³: multiply by 1000 To convert kg/m³ → g/cm³: divide by 1000
writeBullets: ρ = m/V — rearranges to m = ρV and V = m/ρ
Always check units — use consistent units throughout a calculation
1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³ — multiply by 1000 to convert g/cm³ to kg/m³
To check floating: compare calculated density to density of the fluid
If density < fluid density → floats; if density > fluid density → sinks

keyTerms.def: 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³. Multiply by 1000 to convert g/cm³ to kg/m³

keyPoints.memTip: Use a formula triangle: put ρ at the top, m and V at the bottom. Cover what you want to find.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Unit conversion errors are the most common source of lost marks in density questions. Drill this explicitly.

keyPoints.heading: Measuring density in the lab (required practical)
keyPoints.content: There are three experimental situations you need to know for the required practical: 1. Regular solid (e.g., cuboid or cylinder): - Measure dimensions with a ruler (length, width, height for cuboid; radius and height for cylinder) - Calculate volume geometrically: V = l × w × h (cuboid) or V = πr²h (cylinder) - Measure mass using a balance - Calculate: ρ = m/V 2. Irregular solid (e.g., a stone or lump of metal): - Measure mass with a balance - Fill a eureka can (displacement can) to the spout with water, then lower the object in gently - Collect the displaced water in a measuring cylinder → volume of water displaced = volume of object - Alternatively: use a measuring cylinder — record water level, add object, record new level, subtract to find volume - Calculate: ρ = m/V 3. Liquids: - Measure a known volume of liquid using a measuring cylinder - Measure mass of empty container, then with liquid → calculate mass of liquid alone - Calculate: ρ = m/V Sources of error: Parallax error when reading measuring cylinder; not fully submerging the object; using a worn ruler.
writeBullets: Regular solid — measure dimensions with ruler, calculate volume geometrically, use balance for mass
Irregular solid — use eureka can or measuring cylinder displacement to find volume
Volume of displaced water = volume of irregular object (Archimedes' principle)
Liquid — measure known volume with measuring cylinder, find mass with balance
All methods: ρ = m/V after measuring mass and volume separately
Sources of error: parallax, incomplete submersion, worn equipment

keyTerms.def: A displacement can filled to the brim — used to measure the volume of an irregular object by collecting the water it displaces
The volume of liquid pushed aside when an object is submerged — equals the volume of the object

keyPoints.memTip: Regular → ruler and formula. Irregular → water displacement. Liquid → cylinder and balance. Three methods, three situations.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions often ask you to describe the method for an irregular object — make sure you mention collecting the displaced water, not just 'put it in water'.

keyPoints.heading: Density and states of matter
keyPoints.content: The particle model explains why the three states of matter have such different densities: Gases are far less dense than liquids and solids because the particles are very far apart. Most of a gas is empty space. For example, air (~1.2 kg/m³) is about 800 times less dense than water (1000 kg/m³). Solids are usually denser than liquids of the same substance because particles in a solid are more tightly packed. However, there is a famous exception: Water is unusual: Ice (917 kg/m³) is LESS dense than liquid water (1000 kg/m³). This is because in ice, water molecules form a regular open hexagonal lattice held together by hydrogen bonds — this structure actually takes up MORE space than the disordered arrangement in liquid water. This is why: - Ice floats on liquid water - Lakes freeze from the top down (ice forms at the surface, insulating the water below) - Aquatic life can survive under ice in winter Comparing states: - Aluminium solid: 2700 kg/m³ - Liquid aluminium: ~2375 kg/m³ (slightly less dense — particles slightly further apart) - Aluminium vapour: far less dense (particles enormously spread out) The ratio of densities between liquid and gas is typically about 1000:1.
writeBullets: Gases are far less dense than solids and liquids — mostly empty space between particles
Solids are usually denser than liquids of the same substance (particles more tightly packed)
Exception: Ice (917 kg/m³) is LESS dense than liquid water (1000 kg/m³)
Ice forms an open hexagonal lattice — takes up more space than disordered liquid water
Ice floats → lakes freeze from top down → aquatic life protected beneath ice
Ratio of liquid to gas density is typically ~1000:1 (e.g., water vs water vapour)

keyTerms.def: The open ring-shaped structure of ice — water molecules held in a wider arrangement than in liquid water, making ice less dense

keyPoints.memTip: Most solids > their liquid. But water is the exception: ice floats, proving solid water is LESS dense than liquid water.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The water/ice density exception is a favourite exam question — make sure you can explain it using particle arrangement, not just state the fact.

0.question: A stone has a mass of 270 g and a volume of 100 cm³. What is its density in g/cm³?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! ρ = m/V = 270/100 = 2.7 g/cm³. This is the density of aluminium — the stone is denser than water, so it sinks.
0.wrongFeedback: Use ρ = m/V. Divide mass by volume: 270 ÷ 100 = 2.7 g/cm³. Watch out for dividing the wrong way around — mass goes on top.

2.question: Why does ice float on liquid water? Choose the best explanation.
options: Ice is less dense than liquid water because its particles form an open hexagonal lattice that takes up more space
Ice is less dense because its particles have less mass than liquid water particles
Ice floats because it is a solid and all solids float on liquids
Ice floats because it is colder and cold things are always less dense
2.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Ice forms an open hexagonal structure — the particles are actually slightly further apart than in liquid water, making ice less dense (917 vs 1000 kg/m³).
2.wrongFeedback: The particles in ice and liquid water are identical water molecules — same mass. The difference is arrangement. In ice, a hexagonal lattice creates a more open structure, so the same mass takes up more volume → lower density.

3.question: How would you measure the volume of an irregularly shaped lump of rock in the lab?
options: Submerge it in water using a eureka can or measuring cylinder and measure the volume of water displaced
Measure its length, width and height with a ruler and multiply them together
Weigh it on a balance and divide by its known density
Place it on a measuring cylinder and read off the scale
3.correctFeedback: Spot on. For irregular objects you use water displacement — the volume of water pushed out equals the volume of the object.
3.wrongFeedback: You can only use length × width × height for regular shapes like cuboids. For irregular shapes, water displacement (eureka can or measuring cylinder) gives you the volume directly.

commonMistakes: Dividing volume by mass instead of mass by volume — remember ρ = m/V, not V/m.
Forgetting to convert units — mixing grams with m³, or cm³ with kg, gives nonsense answers. Use consistent units throughout.
Thinking all solids are denser than liquids — ice is the classic counterexample: it is less dense than water.
Saying the object 'loses mass' when placed in water — it doesn't; the water displaced has the same volume as the object, not the same mass.
Not subtracting the empty container mass when measuring the density of a liquid.
examTips: Show your formula, substitution, and units clearly in every density calculation — the mark scheme awards marks for each step.
If asked whether an object floats or sinks, calculate its density and compare it directly to the fluid density — state the comparison explicitly.
For the required practical, be ready to describe the method for BOTH regular and irregular objects — state which instrument measures which quantity.
revisionCardBullets: Density equation: ρ = m/V (rho = mass ÷ volume)
Units: kg/m³ or g/cm³. Conversion: 1 g/cm³ = 1000 kg/m³
Water: 1000 kg/m³ (1 g/cm³) — the key reference density
Object floats if density < fluid density; sinks if density > fluid density
Ice (917 kg/m³) is less dense than water (1000 kg/m³) — ice floats
Ice is unusual: its open hexagonal lattice makes it less dense than liquid water
Regular solid: measure dimensions with ruler → calculate volume geometrically
Irregular solid: use eureka can / measuring cylinder displacement
Volume of displaced water = volume of submerged object
Liquid: use measuring cylinder for volume, balance for mass
Gases are far less dense than liquids/solids — mostly empty space
Always use consistent units and show all steps in calculations
